title: These Are the Smart Goggles You Need If You Want to Quickly Improve Your Swimming Form
gist: Form's Smart Swim 2 LT smart swim goggles ($149) extend the company's AR-display swim wearables down-market, but the reviewer flags that core training value (HeadCoach) remains locked behind a $9.99/month subscription, illustrating the hardware-plus-recurring-revenue model in niche wearables.

insights.claim: Form's most useful swim-coaching features, including HeadCoach technique analysis and custom workouts, are gated behind a subscription starting at $9.99/month or $99/year, meaning the $149 hardware alone delivers an incomplete experience.
insights.speaker: Michael Sawh
insights.so_what: Confirms the recurring-revenue playbook in specialized wearables; hardware is the acquisition hook while margin comes from subscriptions, raising the effective cost of ownership.
insights.quote: the fact that you still need to pay for a subscription to access the training and coaching features means these goggles are still going to be out of reach for a lot of swimmers
insights.locator: The Smart Swim 2 LT offers a more affordable way

insights.claim: Form unbundled its SwimStraight open-water compass feature from the Premium subscription tier, making it free on the cheaper LT model.
insights.speaker: Michael Sawh
insights.so_what: Selective freeing of formerly paid features signals competitive pressure on pricing and an attempt to broaden the base for the higher-margin coaching subscription.
insights.quote: This was previously hidden behind Form's Premium subscription.
insights.locator: One open-water feature you're free to access

insights.claim: Form goggles are framed as the swimming analog to Meta's smart glasses, pioneering heads-up AR displays for an underserved swimmer niche despite lacking cameras or speakers.
insights.speaker: Michael Sawh
insights.so_what: Niche AR-display wearables can build defensible product categories away from the saturated smart-glasses fight; watch for incumbents (Garmin, Apple) to encroach.
insights.quote: Form Goggles are like the Meta Glasses of the water.
insights.locator: Form Goggles are like the Meta Glasses

insights.claim: The FINIS Smart Goggles, a direct competitor with a built-in display, have been discontinued, leaving Form with limited direct competition in display-equipped swim goggles.
insights.speaker: Michael Sawh
insights.so_what: Competitor exit gives Form a near-monopoly on the smart-swim-display niche, supporting its premium pricing power.
insights.locator: I've tested other swimming goggles with built-in displays

insights.claim: Form's connected goggles carry a steep premium over regular goggles, with current models at $199-$250 versus $50-$80 for premium Speedo goggles, and the new LT trims cost to $149 by removing the built-in heart-rate monitor and Gorilla Glass lenses.
insights.speaker: Michael Sawh
insights.so_what: Even the budget model commands a 2-3x premium over premium dumb goggles, capping addressable market to committed swimmers and limiting volume scaling.
insights.locator: One glaring problem for Form was and still is the price.

insights.claim: Form's swim tracking now matches the accuracy of Apple Watch and Garmin watches for stroke recognition, lap counts, and distance, and can act as a heads-up display for data relayed from those watches in open water.
insights.speaker: Michael Sawh
insights.so_what: Rather than purely competing with smartwatches, Form positions as a complementary HUD, suggesting interoperability rather than displacement of dominant wearable platforms.
insights.quote: Form's swim tracking is on par with a host of smartwatches and sports watches.
